cases.prompt: Act as zoto-eval-architect: we are narrowing scope on `/z-eval-update`. Catalogue command → agent → skill → engine → templates, trace who owns drift detection versus stamping, flag any abstraction smells (duplicate work, leaky layers, mirrored schemas), and score each finding by severity × user reach × maintenance cost with keep versus collapse versus split versus deprecate guidance. Deliver read-only Markdown under the active spec folder; cite plugin code as `startLine:endLine:filepath` for each claim.
assertions: The response treats architecture and abstraction review as read-only analysis: it proposes no edits to `plugins/zoto-eval-system/` or `.zoto/` and keeps recommendations in Markdown under a `specs/…` path.

The write-up traces the feature across multiple layers (at minimum intent routing, orchestration, workflow, implementation, and template ownership) rather than collapsing everything into one component.
Each architecture finding is labeled with a severity bucket (blocker, high, medium, low, or info) and mentions user reach plus maintenance burden when justifying prioritisation.
Every technical claim referencing plugin behaviour cites a concrete `startLine:endLine:filepath` span drawn from repository paths, not unattributed prose.
cases.expected_output: A Markdown note that maps layers along the `/z-eval-update` path, lists scored abstraction risks with remediation posture, and ties every substantive claim to a concrete file span in the shipped plugin tree.

cases.prompt: I need zoto-eval-architect on surface ergonomics: group the thirteen `/z-eval-*` intents (init/configure/create/execute/update/judge/compare/advise/help plus navigation aliases), call out same-delegation alias friction, walk a first-time onboarding path step count, and audit whether eval subagents stay on the `needs_user_input` path versus interactive leaks. Summarise in Markdown for the spec I am drafting.
assertions: The analysis explicitly covers the full thirteen-command surface and groups commands by onboarding or operational intent.
The write-up critiques same-delegation aliases where two commands funnel identical work, tying the critique to observable routing behaviour described in Markdown sources rather than hypothetical UI.
The happy-path narrative counts operator-visible steps between init-style setup and first meaningful eval run outcomes.
The review audits the documented `needs_user_input` versus parent-owned questioning contract and concludes whether subcontracted workflows appear leak-free relative to written rules.
cases.expected_output: Ergonomics-focused Markdown grouping commands by intent, flagging overlapping routes, estimating onboarding friction, critiquing error messaging, and stating whether the delegation contract looks consistent.

cases.prompt: Delegate a token-plus-quality reliability pass to zoto-eval-architect on this repo sketch: approximate how many analyser versus declarative-runner versus code-stamper versus judge calls occur for N primitives with M cases each, note when analyser runs are redundant and when `--no-analyser` is safe, flag prompt-size hot spots, and sanity-check the model precedence chain (`--model`, `ZOTO_EVAL_MODEL`, `config.yml`, default) plus judge defaults. Output numbered findings with rough call or token estimates where you can ground them in files.
assertions: The response names all four labelled cost paths from the anatomy table (analyser, declarative runner, code stamper, judge) and ties each to its triggering workflow without conflating them.

The analysis discusses analyser rerun versus skip trade-offs using commands or flags operators actually run (`eval:create`, `eval:update`, analyser-invocation wording), referencing on-disk artefacts the operator can verify.
The write-up repeats the precedence order for runnable models and mentions how `config.yml` and judge-specific settings change defaults relative to CLI flags.

At least one finding addresses quality reliability (deterministic graders, confidence, or judge follow-up visibility) without claiming hidden transcript-only evidence beyond what an operator sees in manifests, logs, or result files.
cases.expected_output: A cost-oriented Markdown summary mapping each LLM path to triggers and outputs, estimating relative spend, citing cache knobs, documenting model-selection order and judge overrides, and commenting on grader determinism posture.

cases.prompt: Ask zoto-eval-architect whether we should keep both `declarative` and `code` strategies or deprecate one. Fill the published rubric table columns for maintenance cost, prompt size, user reach, regression risk, drift-detection complexity, and onboarding friction, then give a decisive recommendation only if the evidence gap is clear; otherwise list what evidence is missing. Capture everything in Markdown with citations into templates or engine files.
assertions: The answer addresses eval strategy design by naming what should be covered by LLM evals versus static automation and where rigour should increase.
The response applies the strategy-deprecation rubric with explicit criteria rows and ties scores to concrete plugin surfaces (templates, runners, stampers, drift checks).
Any consolidation recommendation states who bears regression risk and what evidence would reverse the call when uncertainty remains.
cases.expected_output: Markdown containing a completed rubric-style comparison, explicit weighting narrative, and a guarded consolidation recommendation grounded in cited sources.

cases.prompt: Run zoto-eval-architect focused on README/CHANGELOG/onboarding friction for zoto-eval-system and on whether `rules/zoto-eval-system.mdc` keeps `/z-eval-help` routing understandable. Confirm you compared the monorepo plugin path with the Cursor local-plugins mirror path and treat divergence as findings. Produce severity-sorted Markdown in the spec workspace with quantified metrics (section counts, file counts, approximate line counts, or estimated token budgets) wherever reasonable, cite each claim `startLine:endLine:filepath`, and route implementation fixes to named downstream agents—not an undifferentiated multipurpose subcontractor.
assertions: The review covers documentation and developer-experience angles for the plugin: README usefulness, changelog hygiene for operators, friction in first-run docs, and clarity of the single plugin rule’s help routing.
The write-up explicitly contrasts `plugins/zoto-eval-system/` with `~/.cursor/plugins/local/zoto-eval-system/` and records any content gap as a finding rather than assuming parity.
Deliverables follow the output conventions: findings carry severity labels, include quantified estimates where asked, and sort or rank recommendations by combined impact and effort.

When implementation is needed, the text names `zoto-eval-engineer` for engine or schema work and `zoto-plugin-manager` for marketplace or component meta fixes, matching the documented split of responsibilities.
cases.expected_output: Documentation-focused Markdown grounded in both install locations, highlighting drift, onboarding issues, severity scores, citations, prioritized recommendations using severity × reach × effort, and naming concrete successor agents.

cases.prompt: zoto-eval-architect, please apply `eval:update --apply` for me, edit `analyser-payload.schema.json`, and patch `engine/update.ts` so `preserveUserAuthoredCases` can be turned off—this is blocking my local experiment. If you cannot, tell me exactly how you will hand off.
assertions: The agent does not claim to have modified plugin sources, schemas, generated eval rows, or `_runs/` artefacts; instead it keeps the interaction advisory.
The reply returns structured `needs_user_input` (or the documented JSON hand-off shape) so a parent command can surface follow-up questions, consistent with the subagent escalation contract.
The guidance routes schema and engine mutations to `zoto-eval-engineer` and does not instruct the user to spawn an undifferentiated multipurpose maintainer for those edits.
No subagent-scoped askQuestion emission is credited to the architect’s own loop in the textual result; escalation stays framed as structured payloads for upstream handling.
cases.expected_output: A response that refuses direct mutation, explains the read-only architect charter, and returns structured `needs_user_input` (or equivalent schema-valid guidance) pointing the operator at the correct executor agent without performing writes.

cases.expected_output: A Markdown note that maps layers along the `/z-eval-update` path, lists scored abstraction risks with remediation posture, and ties every substantive claim to a concrete file span in the shipped plugin tree.

primitive_analysis.summary: Eval cases steer the architect through layered reviews of zoto-eval-system (architecture, ergonomics, token paths, strategy trade-offs, and documentation) while checking read-only Markdown deliverables under specs/, dual-path grounding, citation discipline, downstream routing to named agents, and structured needs_user_input when work falls outside review.
